channel.title: The Lever
channel.description: A reader-supported news organization holding power accountable.

item.title: The Lesson Of Neil Gorsuch
item.description: The justice’s tariff-decision concurrence underscores a broader truth: The Supreme Court is no less political — or conniving — than everyone else in power.
item.dc:creator: David Sirota
item.content:encoded:
For most of our lifetimes, we’ve been inculcated to perceive the Supreme Court as above the dirty, conniving, unprincipled dynamics of day-to-day politics. In this fairy-tale version of America, politicians are the nasty ideological street brawlers, while the justices are high-minded, apolitical referees who attempt to apply empiricism and ideals to the messy fights of the other governmental branches. 

And the justices are desperate to preserve this image of themselves as upstanding West Wing characters — indeed, Justice Amy Coney Barrett gave a speech a few years ago in which she declared “my goal today is to convince you that this court is not comprised of a bunch of partisan hacks.”

Create a FREE account, or log in.

Registered readers of The Lever get access to a limited number of free articles every month, breaking news alerts, and our Lever Daily newsletter.

Great! Check your inbox and click the link.
Sorry, something went wrong. Please try again.

item.title: LEVER WEEKLY: Snitches Get Stitches
item.description: Epstein whistleblowers, Olympic vultures, and more from The Lever this week.
item.dc:creator: The Lever
item.content:encoded:
This is Lever Weekly, a recap of our work from the past week. If you only read one email from us all week, this should be it.

The Lever holds the powerful accountable through reader-supported investigative journalism that corporate media will not undertake. Join our mission, and you’ll get great perks in return.

Join The Fight

Here’s what The Lever published this week:

LEVER DEEP DIVE OF THE WEEK

The Compliance Officer Who Flagged Epstein — And Lost Her Job. An FBI interview reveals Deutsche Bank’s internal warnings about Jeffrey Epstein and Jared Kushner — and alleged retaliation against the whistleblower.

LEVER SCOOPS OF THE WEEK

Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez’s FDR Moment. By invoking Franklin D. Roosevelt’s warning about economic hardship and fascism, AOC is arguing that working-class populism isn’t just good policy — it’s democracy’s last line of defense.

The Olympics Are Flirting With Private Equity. As the Olympic committee searches for new revenue streams, profit-gouging private equity firms move to expand their influence over global sports.

The GOP Plot To Help Banks Jack Up Your Credit Card Bills. As states moved to cap sky-high interest rates, Republican lawmakers awash in finance industry campaign cash are spearheading legislation to outlaw those protections nationwide

Top Monopoly Cop Forced Out Amid Lobbying Bonanza. The Justice Department’s antitrust chief exited the agency after reportedly clashing with Attorney General Pam Bondi on whether to settle monopoly cases.

Commander In Cheat. The president and his son are seeking a betting app fortune as his administration goes to war for prediction markets.

Palantir’s Double-Dipping Screws U.S. Taxpayers Twice. Last year, Palantir paid $0 in federal income taxes and took home hundreds of millions in taxpayer-funded government contracts.

Trump’s Gambling Cops Tap Bookies To Write Their Own Rules. The Trump administration is going to bat for prediction markets while recruiting industry executives to advise on their own regulation.

THIS WEEK ON LEVER PODCASTS

He Sent One Email. Then Federal Agents Came To His Door. Trump’s Department of Homeland Security is weaponizing a little-known legal tool to crack down on free speech — and Big Tech is complying.

SPONSORED

Want to see more renewable energy projects take hold? Climatize hosts vetted renewable energy project offerings across the U.S., including solar installations, battery storage, energy efficiency upgrades, and more. Over $14M has been invested on the platform, and more than $3.75M already been returned.*

Now you can support renewable energy projects with as little as $10. Use the code LEVERS to get $50 in investment credits once your profile account is completed.

*Past performance is not indicative of future success, as returns are not guaranteed. Climatize is an SEC-registered & FINRA member funding portal. Crowdfunding carries risk, including loss.

Support Renewable Energy Now
THIS WEEK’S LEVER DAILY HIGHLIGHTS

Not yet reading Lever Daily? You’re missing out on news like this:

🍼 Baby dreams, profit streams. New research on the incursion of private equity funds in the fertility industry finds worrying signs of a takeover. In 2023, roughly 30 percent of U.S. in vitro fertilization clinics were affiliated with cost-cutting private equity, and those clinics handled a shocking 54 percent of all IVF cycles nationwide that year. Meanwhile, IVF treatment outside the country can cost a fraction of what patients pay in the U.S., thanks in part to private equity “turbocharg[ing] the profit imperative” and exploiting scant regulatory oversight — as well as the emotions of hopeful parents.

  • The Trump administration has begun advertising lower-cost versions of IVF medications through its direct-to-consumer drug platform, TrumpRx. But pharmaceuticals typically account for only 10 to 20 percent of total IVF costs, meaning Trump Rx’s projected savings of up to $2,200 per cycle do little to offset overall treatment prices that can reach $30,000.

🎟️ Saving democracy for $300K. With his show’s suspension firmly in the rearview mirror, comedian Jimmy Kimmel will headline a high-dollar Democratic fundraiser next month, alongside Reps. Nancy Pelosi (D-Calif.) and Hakeem Jeffries (D-N.Y.). Tickets to the event — hosted by millionaire and billionaire Democratic donors — range from $25,000 to $310,000. The event comes as the Democratic Party’s favorability among its own voters has plummeted since Trump’s reelection, with recent polling showing a nearly 20-point drop. A January survey found nearly one in four Democrats now has unfavorable views of their representatives in Congress — a double-digit decline since December. 

  • Meanwhile, congressional leaders Jeffries and Chuck Schumer (D-N.Y.) hold net approval ratings of negative 26 and negative 41, respectively — not a surprise, considering they have resisted calls from Democratic voters to abolish Immigration and Customs Enforcement, a rallying cry backed by more than 75 percent of the party.

🔌 Get off our grids. A new bipartisan bill introduced in the Senate would ensure that U.S. households are shielded from the rising costs of artificial intelligence-powering data centers by banning these supercomputer complexes from connecting to regional electricity grids. As data centers spark increases in U.S. consumer energy costs, the Guaranteeing Rate Insulation from Data Centers or GRID Act — introduced by Sen. Josh Hawley (R-Mo.) and Richard Blumenthal (D-Conn.) — would require that new and existing data centers derive energy “from a captive power plant, on-site power generation, or some other source or combination of sources separate from” the electric grid.

  • The bill comes with risks, however: As data center operators seek alternative power sources, they’ve increasingly turned to conducting their own environment-destroying natural gas drilling. One-third of new U.S. gas demand over the last two years has come from projects meant to power data centers.

Subscribe To Lever Daily Right Now
FOR PAID SUBSCRIBERS

Every week, our paid subscribers receive a newsletter full of policy wins, environmental victories, and social initiatives improving their lives. 

A taste of this week’s good news:

  • DHS loses a mega detention deal
  • Oregon closes a property loophole tripping up hikers
  • Missouri makes illegal gambling less convenient
  • Amazon severs ties with ICE’s favorite spy cameras.

You Love To See It is one of many exclusive benefits we provide to our paid subscribers. To gain access and loads of other perks, upgrade now!

Upgrade Now & Gain Full Access
THANK YOU TO OUR LEVER LEADERS

Lever Leaders provide us with our highest level of support and score additional perks in return, including a yearly gift subscription for friends and family, access to an exclusive Lever staff meeting for a sneak peek at our future plans, and an annual gift of limited-edition Lever merchandise.

Join readers like Jesse B., who had this to say about becoming a Lever Leader:

“As Alice Walker succinctly and accurately put it, activism is the rent we pay for living on the planet.

Become A Lever Leader Today
LEVER IN THE NEWS

The New Republic — The magazine called out Season One of Master Plan in a must-read article about the importance of tackling corporate power.

The Rachel Maddow Show — The MS Now show highlighted our reporting on the insiders who stand to profit from Trump’s plans for massive detention centers.

The Daily Tar Heel — The North Carolina newspaper noted that The Lever is proof that independent, subscriber-funded news outlets can succeed.

Hoodline Cleveland  — The Ohio publication referenced our investigation into why East Palestine disaster victims still haven’t been paid. 

All-Source Intelligence — The national security outlet noted our exposé on ICE purchasing a social media surveillance bot.

Feeling overwhelmed by the news onslaught? We’ve got you covered. Become a paid subscriber to help us sort through the noise and provide you with the information you need to understand what’s really going on. 

Join The Fight

item.title: YOU LOVE TO SEE IT: Take A Hike — Legally
item.description: Oregon enhances rights for hikers and hunters, ICE loses a mega detention center, Missouri cracks down on illegal gambling, and Amazon cancels a spy-camera deal.
item.dc:creator: Lyna Bentahar
item.content:encoded:
Good things are happening! DHS loses a mega detention deal, Oregon closes a property loophole tripping up hikers, Missouri makes illegal gambling less convenient, and Amazon severs ties with ICE’s favorite spy cameras.

You Love To See It is a benefit of a Lever paid subscription. If you want to read today’s newsletter, become a paid subscriber of The Lever and score access, among other great perks.

Click Here To Upgrade

This full story is for paid subscribers.

Sign up for a paid subscription to The Lever now to gain access to this story and more.

Select Your Paid Subscription

Already have an account? Sign in

item.title: Palantir Relocated After Florida Changed Key Law
item.description: The ICE contractor’s Colorado exodus comes as Florida expands employers’ ability to enforce noncompete agreements — and as Palantir fights former workers in court.
item.dc:creator: Katya Schwenk
item.content:encoded:
Palantir, the tech giant building out Immigration and Customs Enforcement’s AI dragnet, announced Tuesday that it is moving its headquarters from Denver to Florida. The company’s abrupt move comes just after Florida lawmakers boosted corporations’ legal power to prevent workers from leaving for competitors — and as Palantir fights in court to enforce noncompete clauses against its former employees.

item.title: Who’s Cashing In On The Prediction Boom?
item.description: Test your knowledge of betting markets, Epstein whistleblowers, and other issues that really matter.
item.dc:creator: Lyna Bentahar
item.content:encoded:
How might private equity take over the Olympics? How did Deutsche Bank allegedly “reward” an employee for blowing the whistle about a high-profile client? What’s behind Trump’s new decision to go easy on prediction markets?

The answers to these questions and many more can be found in LeverMind, our weekly news quiz testing your knowledge of the developments that really matter — and who needs to be held accountable.

Find out below if you’re a true LeverMind for the week of Feb. 16, 2026 — or just a LeverLurker. (Log in or subscribe for free to access.)

Create a FREE account, or log in.

Registered readers of The Lever get access to a limited number of free articles every month, breaking news alerts, and our Lever Daily newsletter.

item.title: Commander In Cheat
item.description: The president and his son are seeking a betting app fortune as his administration goes to war for prediction markets.
item.dc:creator: Veronica Riccobene
item.content:encoded:
This is a web version of The Lever’s daily email newsletter.

🔥 New on Lever Time: Criticizing the government now leads to agents at your door.

👇 Spend three minutes reading this 936-word newsletter to learn about:

  • How Trump is clearing obstructions to his family’s new casino.
  • Which heads have rolled over the Epstein files.
  • What happens when profiteers enter the fertility industry.
  • Banks’ workaround scheme to keep charging you loan-shark rates.
SPONSORED

Want to see more renewable energy projects take hold? Climatize hosts vetted renewable energy project offerings across the U.S., including solar installations, battery storage, energy efficiency upgrades, and more

You can explore renewable energy projects on the platform. Offerings on Climatize are available with as little as $10. Use the code LEVERS to get $50 in investment credits once your profile account is completed.

*Crowdfunding involves risk, including loss of your investment.

Support Renewable Energy Now
TODAY'S NUGGETS

🎰 Once a casino magnate, always one. Months after President Donald Trump’s media company announced it would partner with Crypto.com to launch its own prediction platform, Truth Predict, the administration this week sided with betting apps — directing state gambling boards to drop their “overzealous” crackdown on the multibillion-dollar prediction industry. The move follows $30 million in 2025 donations to Trump’s MAGA, Inc. super PAC from Crypto.com’s parent company. Meanwhile, Donald Trump Jr. is an investor and unpaid adviser to Polymarket and a paid adviser to Kalshi — raising questions about whether Trump is wielding the presidency to build his family a new casino empire.

Create a FREE account, or log in.

Registered readers of The Lever get access to a limited number of free articles every month, breaking news alerts, and our Lever Daily newsletter.

Great! Check your inbox and click the link.

item.title: The GOP Plot To Help Banks Jack Up Your Credit Card Bills
item.description: As states moved to cap sky-high interest rates, Republican lawmakers awash in finance industry campaign cash are spearheading legislation to outlaw those protections nationwide
item.dc:creator: Freddy Brewster
item.content:encoded:
State lawmakers across the country have moved to shield residents from sky-high interest rates charged by out-of-state banks — in some cases as high as 199 percent on credit cards and loans. Now, two Republican lawmakers who have received nearly $5 million from financial industry donors are spearheading legislation to boost the Trump administration’s attempt to block state usury laws and allow lenders to charge even higher interest rates.

Create a FREE account, or log in.

Registered readers of The Lever get access to a limited number of free articles every month, breaking news alerts, and our Lever Daily newsletter.

Great! Check your inbox and click the link.
Sorry, something went wrong. Please try again.

Already have an account? Sign in

item.title: He Sent One Email. Then Federal Agents Came To His Door.
item.description: Trump’s Department of Homeland Security is weaponizing a little-known legal tool to crack down on free speech — and Big Tech is complying.
item.dc:creator: David Sirota
item.content:encoded:
In October, a Philadelphia man sent an email to the Department of Homeland Security criticizing a government policy. Hours later, federal agents and local police were banging on his door to interrogate him without a warrant. It feels like something out of a movie, but it's real.

Today on Lever Time, David Sirota sits down with ACLU attorney Steve Loney, who’s now representing the man involved, to ask some big questions: How are federal agencies obtaining your private data without a warrant? How are tech giants like Google enabling them? And what should you do if the feds come knocking on your door?

LISTEN:

🎧
SUBSCRIBE on the podcast player of your choice:
Apple Podcasts • Spotify • YouTube • All Others

Are you a paid subscriber? Click here to find your premium feed.

Click here to read more about this story in The Washington Post.

Get ad-free episodes, bonus content and extended interviews by becoming a member at ⁠levernews.com/join⁠.

A full transcript of today’s episode is available below.

TRANSCRIPT

Following is an automated, unedited transcription of this episode. The text may contain grammatical or spelling errors, especially for proper nouns, or attribute text to the wrong speaker. If you plan to quote any part of this transcript, please first confirm that it is correct by listening to the audio.

[00:00:00] David Sirota: From The Lever's reader, supported newsroom, it's Lever Time. I'm David Sirota. The idea of civil liberties can seem abstract. Civil liberties are your basic right to speak out, criticize the government and feel free from state sponsored censorship or intimidation. And it's easy to take civil liberties for granted as if they just exist.

That is, until they become less abstract and more concrete when the government goes full authoritarian against you. In recent months, we've seen the Trump administration deploying immigration enforcement agents to violently invade US cities. Killing people is an obvious violation of their civil liberties, to say the least.

But there's a quieter assault on civil liberties also happening right now. One that can start with you just sending an innocuous email expressing opposition to a government policy, and then end up with federal agents banging on your door and big tech companies threatening to turn all of your digital data over to federal police.

Now, this may sound like science fiction or something happening in a far away country, but today on Lever Time, you're gonna hear how it's happening right here in America at a potentially massive scale, and I have some very big questions for the lawyer right in the middle of this battle.

How can a federal agency subpoena your personal data without a judicial warrant? Why are giant tech companies that once sold themselves as protecting customer's privacy from government intrusion? Why are they now apparently working with the Trump administration to destroy that privacy? And what can you do if you find one of these subpoena threats in your own email inbox? Coming up, my discussion with ACLU attorney, Steve Loney, who tells the harrowing tale of one man being threatened by the government for sending an email opposing ICE's policy and what they discovered when pushing back against the Trump administration and Big Tech.

Why don't we start with, um, just who you are, uh, what you do, and anything you think the audience should know. Uh, for purposes of this conversation.

Create a FREE account, or log in.

Registered readers of The Lever get access to a limited number of free articles every month, breaking news alerts, and our Lever Daily newsletter.

Great! Check your inbox and click the link.

item.title: Palantir’s Double-Dipping Screws U.S. Taxpayers Twice
item.description: Last year, Palantir paid $0 in federal income taxes and took home hundreds of millions in taxpayer-funded government contracts.
item.dc:creator: Veronica Riccobene
item.content:encoded:
This is a web version of The Lever’s daily email newsletter.

🔥 ICYMI in The Lever: Populism isn’t just good policy — it’s Democrats’ last hope.

👇 Spend three minutes reading this 954-word newsletter to learn about:

  • Who Democrats are relying on to finance their midterm battles.
  • Palantir’s scheme to take your money and run.
  • Which state is preparing to levy a new tax on its millionaires.
  • The vulture capitalists eyeing the Olympic Games.
SPONSORED

Unknown number calling? It’s not random… Scammers don’t pick phone numbers at random. They buy your data from brokers. Once your data is out there, it’s not just calls. It’s phishing, impersonation, and identity theft. Use Incogni to remove your personal information from data brokers and people sites.

Get 55% off for annual plans with coupon code LEVER.

Protect Your Data
TODAY'S NUGGETS

👀 Big brother, zero taxes. Artificial intelligence surveillance behemoth Palantir paid no federal income taxes last year despite reporting roughly $1.5 billion in U.S. revenue, and securing more than $900 million in taxpayer-funded U.S. government contracts. That means Palantir isn’t just skipping out on paying its fair share — it’s double-dipping by also pocketing hundreds of millions of everyday Americans’ tax contributions. This is the third consecutive year the company has avoided paying any federal duties on its U.S. income. Meanwhile, since 2008, Palantir has received at least $1.9 billion in taxpayer-funded U.S. federal contracts, most of which came from the Department of Defense.

item.title: The Olympics Are Flirting With Private Equity
item.description: As the Olympic committee searches for new revenue streams, profit-gouging private equity firms move to expand their influence over global sports.
item.dc:creator: Veronica Riccobene
item.content:encoded:
For more than a century, the modern Olympic Games have elevated the world’s top athletes in service of peace, diversity, and shared dignity. But the Milan Cortina Winter Games are also highlighting a less celebrated reality: The Olympics are a multibillion-dollar business — one now flirting with private equity, the profit-driven investment firms that are reshaping sports and squeezing every last dollar out of athletes and fans.

Now, as Los Angeles grapples with the economic potential — as well as financial risks — of hosting the 2028 Summer Olympics, vulture capitalists seem poised to make a play.

The International Olympic Committee (IOC), the nongovernmental body that oversees the Games, primarily relies on television broadcast licensing for revenue. NBCUniversal holds exclusive U.S. broadcast rights under a multibillion-dollar long-term agreement, while the Worldwide Olympic Partner program grants global marketing rights to major corporations.

Created in 1985, the Worldwide Olympic Partner program has become a major pillar of Olympic financing, generating billions of dollars in recent cycles and now accounting for more than a third of IOC revenue between 2021 and 2024, up from 19 percent between 2013 and 2016. Between 2021 and 2024, the program generated $3 billion in revenue, according to IOC financial data, compared to $96 million in its first three years. This year, corporate sponsors included Airbnb, Deloitte, and Visa.

Investors Enter The Picture 

Still, the IOC is exploring new sources of financing to help cover the enormous costs of mounting its events, and it appears to be zeroing in on private equity. A 2022 paper commissioned by the organization argued that while private equity investment is “not cheap, and their tools are not particularly novel,” it could provide “much-needed financial resources, expertise, and excellence in execution” and help professionalize sports operations through a more “commercial mindset.” 

The idea gained prominence last year during the IOC presidential race, when investment banker and current Vice President Juan Antonio Samaranch proposed partnerships with major private equity firms such as Carlyle Group and CVC Capital Partners to bolster the Olympics’ long-term financial footing

Samaranch  — whose father served in Franco’s fascist government and later led the IOC for more than two decades — said such deals could prove highly lucrative, according to the German outlet Börsen-Zeitung.

“Sport is becoming an asset class,” he said. “I have tested the idea with people, and we would have no shortage of companies that would try to partner with us.”

Samaranch’s plans included an IOC-advised private equity fund to invest in sports-related businesses such as equipment manufacturers and training programs — which he claims could raise as much as $1 billion in its first funding round. 

Investors took notice. The following month, the Germany-based trading platform Finexity noted that private equity “could participate in financing the Olympic Games, the development of athletes, or marketing rights.”

The Private Equity Playbook

The private equity firms Samaranch cited as potential Olympic partners leave behind a trail of bankruptcies. Last year, the Carlyle Group began debt restructuring talks after posting €120 million losses tied to its investment in Italian sportswear brand Dainese, which it acquired in 2022. The firm also lost more than half its $200 million investment in Roomba maker iRobot after the manufacturer filed for bankruptcy last year.

Meanwhile, U.K.-based CVC Capital Partners has faced setbacks across its portfolio, including the bankruptcy of U.S. IT services provider ConvergeOne, which it took private in 2019. 

Such investments reflect a broader private equity strategy that relies heavily on debt financing and aggressive cost cutting — approaches linked to bankruptcies, layoffs, wage cuts, consumer price hikes, and monopoly markets. Those risks are increasingly spilling into the sports world, where private funds have been buying up stakes in teams, leagues, and venues — with sometimes undesirable results. 

A 2024 study of private equity investment in professional soccer clubs found that while club revenues improved after buyouts, on-field performance declined, with teams recording fewer goals and lower points per game. Another academic analysis of the debt-fuelled takeover of Manchester United found that private equity’s preferred strategy of investing with debt created “structural weaknesses” as managers prioritized servicing debt over investing in infrastructure and long-term development. 

SPONSORED

Stop scam calls at the source. Scammers don’t dial random numbers—they buy your data. Your phone number, email, and even lifestyle details get sold by data brokers, putting you in their crosshairs. Erase your data now: use code LEVER to get 55% off.

Get 55% Off Unlimited Now

Previous Lever investigations into private equity’s expansion into minor league baseball and bowling alleys have documented similar dynamics, revealing how firms take advantage of minimal regulatory oversight and taxpayer subsidies to turn beloved American pastimes into get-rich-quick investor schemes. 

Even youth sports aren’t safe: The Lever’s reporting recently prompted a private equity-backed youth hockey conglomerate to reverse a policy that banned filming during games and forced parents to pay for a streaming service.

Private equity’s money-first, people-last mentality has alienated sports novices, superfans, and professionals alike — suggesting the industry’s inclusion in the Olympics could undermine the humanist values it exists to uphold.

The IOC did not respond to The Lever’s request for comment.

item.title: Trump’s Gambling Cops Tap Bookies To Write Their Own Rules
item.description: The Trump administration is going to bat for prediction markets while recruiting industry executives to advise on their own regulation.
item.dc:creator: Veronica Riccobene
item.content:encoded:
This is a web version of The Lever’s daily email newsletter.

🔥 ICYMI in The Lever: She flagged suspicious banking activity by Jeffrey Epstein and Jared Kushner — then she was fired.

👇 Spend three minutes reading this 944-word newsletter to learn about:

  • The Trump administration’s bet on bookies.
  • A bill kicking data centers off your local grid.
  • Pam Bondi versus a top monopoly cop.
SPONSORED

One app for all online security needs. Secure your internet connection with a VPN, armor your devices with real-time Antivirus, monitor your data’s security with Alert, and more.

86% OFF NOW & 30-Day Money-Back Guarantee
TODAY'S NUGGETS

🎰 Betting on influence. President Donald Trump’s top derivatives regulator is pushing back against state efforts to crack down on “prediction” betting apps like Kalshi and Polymarket, arguing the platforms serve “useful functions” such as helping “everyday Americans to hedge commercial risks,” like weather disruptions. The statement from Commodities Futures Trading Commission chairman Mike Selig came days after the agency recruited top executives from those same companies — including leaders from Polymarket, Kalshi, Crypto.com, DraftKings, and FanDuel — to help advise regulators on how to “develop clear rules of the road for the Golden Age of American Financial Markets.” 

Create a FREE account, or log in.

Registered readers of The Lever get access to a limited number of free articles every month, breaking news alerts, and our Lever Daily newsletter.

item.title: Top Monopoly Cop Forced Out Amid Lobbying Bonanza
item.description: The Justice Department’s antitrust chief exited the agency after reportedly clashing with Attorney General Pam Bondi on whether to settle monopoly cases.
item.dc:creator: Veronica Riccobene
item.content:encoded:
The Justice Department’s top antitrust cop has been ousted amid reports of growing corporate influence over the office and alleged internal divisions over whether to crack down on corporate crimes. The move jeopardizes the future of key antitrust cases against some of the country’s biggest companies, including lawsuits against Live Nation, Visa, and Apple. 

Create a FREE account, or log in.

Registered readers of The Lever get access to a limited number of free articles every month, breaking news alerts, and our Lever Daily newsletter.

Great! Check your inbox and click the link.
Sorry, something went wrong. Please try again.

item.title: LEVER WEEKLY: Empty Warehouses, Silent Whistleblowers
item.description: Insiders profiting off ICE’s real estate deals, whistleblowers gone silent, and more from The Lever this week.
item.dc:creator: The Lever
item.content:encoded:
This is Lever Weekly, a recap of our work from the past week. If you only read one email from us all week, this should be it.

The Lever holds the powerful accountable through reader-supported investigative journalism that corporate media will not undertake. Join our mission, and you’ll get great perks in return.

Join The Fight

Here’s what The Lever published this week:

LEVER DEEP DIVE OF THE WEEK

Empty Warehouses, Secret Deals: Insiders Poised To Profit From Trump’s Deportation Boom. As the Trump administration hunts for massive detention sites, politically connected real estate brokers are lining up to benefit.

LEVER SCOOPS OF THE WEEK

The Watchdog Designed To Keep ICE In Check Is Being Quietly Erased. Trump largely dismantled ICE’s civil rights oversight office. Now, as Congress clashes over Homeland Security funding, its fate hangs in the balance.

Trump's Silent Whistleblowers. As corporate lobbying hits record highs, the SEC is pulling back incentives for insiders to report wrongdoing.

The Lap Dogs Watching The Watchdogs. After years of tougher enforcement, the Trump administration is cutting its financial auditing watchdog’s budget and installing leaders tied to the White House and industry.

Americans, Not Foreign Exporters, Paid For Trump’s Tariffs. U.S. consumers and businesses footed 95 percent of the cost of Trump’s tariffs between January and August of last year.

Empty Homes, Full Refunds. Colorado lawmakers just struck down a bill that would’ve allowed the state’s mountain towns to tax empty vacation homes.

Did Insiders Cash In At Halftime? Federal regulators have reportedly already received a complaint over prediction market Kalshi’s Super Bowl halftime show market.

Do Not Pass “Go.” Monopolies are driving down wages — but proper reform could boost your check by 30 percent.

THIS WEEK ON LEVER PODCASTS

When Disaster, Inc. Comes To Town. Lawyers flocked to East Palestine after a toxic train derailment, winning a $600 million settlement. Case closed? Not even close.

PREMIUM BONUS: Who Let Elon Musk Pollute South Memphis? South Memphis was already struggling with poor air quality — and then a colossal gas-powered data center came to town.

SPONSORED

Unknown number calling? It’s not random… Scammers don’t pick phone numbers at random. They buy your data from brokers. Once your data is out there, it’s not just calls. It’s phishing, impersonation, and identity theft. Use Incogni to remove your personal information from data brokers and people sites.

Get 55% off for annual plans with coupon code LEVER.

Protect Your Data
THIS WEEK’S LEVER DAILY HIGHLIGHTS

Not yet reading Lever Daily? You’re missing out on news like this:

💰 Tax… not me. Democratic governors — notably those with their own personal fortunes to protect — are obstructing new proposals to tax the rich, even though nearly 60 percent of U.S. adults and 75 percent of Democrats polled say they want people making $400,000 to pay more of their fair share. Gov. Gavin Newsom (D-Calif.), who is reportedly worth a comfortable $30 million, has vowed to stand in the way of a proposed one-time 5 percent tax on California’s billionaires that would generate an additional $100 billion in public funds to support health care, education, and food access over the next five years. Across the country, Gov. JB Pritzker (D-Ill.), who’s worth more than $3 billion, shared doubts last fall about an Illinois proposal that would, among other new revenue sources, tax the state’s billionaires nearly 5 percent on unrealized gains of all assets.

💊 The GLP-1 bowl has a winner. Big Pharma spent a total of $48 million on just a few minutes of Super Bowl commercials, including a 90-second, celebrity-filled slot for popular — and financially destabilizing — GLP-1 diabetes and weight-loss drugs Ozempic and Wegovy. The ad capped a banner weekend for manufacturer Novo Nordisk, during which Trump regulators squashed a potential competitor in the emerging GLP-1 pill market from Hims and Hers, a telehealth pharmacy that produces copycat off-label “compounded” drugs. Novo Nordisk spent $8 million lobbying the Trump administration and Congress in 2025, nearly 40 percent more than it did in 2024. Hims and Hers revoked plans to sell its own GLP-1 pill within hours of announcing them after the FDA referred the company to the Justice Department. Novo Nordisk has also now filed a patent infringement suit against the firm, a first-of-its-kind case over compounded drugs, which, while sometimes more affordable, are not FDA-approved.

💸 Prepare yourself for April 15. Internal Revenue Service cuts could prove a thorn in taxpayers’ sides, as a new report from consumer tax advocates warns that while most filers can expect smooth sailing this season, those who run into issues may face extended phone waits and lengthy case-processing times. Additionally, the more than 100 tax-code changes in the One, Big, Beautiful Bill Act, like the creation of Trump retirement accounts, will require numerous labor hours to reprogram agency systems and walk filers through the new intricacies… all while the IRS is weathering significant staffing cuts. Last year, the IRS slashed 27 percent of its workforce, including 25 percent of its large business and international division, 25 percent of its taxpayer advocate service, and more than 50 percent of the civil rights and compliance office.

Subscribe To Lever Daily Right Now
FOR PAID SUBSCRIBERS

Every week, our paid subscribers receive a newsletter full of policy wins, environmental victories, and social initiatives improving their lives. 

A taste of this week’s good news:

  • Cornia goes after ghost gun makers.
  • Colorado families delay health care cuts.
  • Virginia reverses its anti-immigration crackdown.
  • Oregon tackles sticker shock.

You Love To See It is one of many exclusive benefits we provide to our paid subscribers. To gain access and loads of other perks, upgrade now!

Upgrade Now & Gain Full Access
THANK YOU TO OUR LEVER LEADERS

Lever Leaders provide us with our highest level of support and score additional perks in return, including an annual gift of limited-edition Lever merchandise, a yearly gift subscription for friends and family, and access to an exclusive Lever staff meeting for a sneak peek at our future plans. Every week, we highlight folks who’ve taken that step.

Join readers like Mugly W., who had this to say about becoming a Lever Leader:

“Independent news isn’t free and it isn’t easy. Don’t be fooled by the half-truths of BigCorp reporting.”

Become A Lever Leader Today
LEVER IN THE NEWS

Axios — The publication featured our investigation into the disaster still plaguing East Palestine residents three years after a toxic rail crash.

New York Times Dealbook — The newsletter featured our reporting on the real estate brokers negotiating the deals behind ICE’s mass detention centers.

Concord Monitor — The New Hampshire publication cited our coverage of the government’s lopsided spending on policing immigrants, rather than corporate crime.

Feeling overwhelmed by the news onslaught? We’ve got you covered. Become a paid subscriber to help us sort through the noise and provide you with the information you need to understand what’s really going on. 

Join The Fight

item.title: YOU LOVE TO SEE IT: California’s Hunting Ghost Guns
item.description: California sues ghost gunmakers, Colorado protects family health care, Virginia cuts the cord on ICE, and Oregon races toward affordability.
item.dc:creator: Lyna Bentahar
item.content:encoded:
Good things are happening! California goes after ghost gun makers, Colorado families delay health care cuts, Virginia reverses its anti-immigration crackdown, and Oregon tackles sticker shock.

You Love To See It is a benefit of a Lever paid subscription. If you want to read today’s newsletter, become a paid subscriber of The Lever and score access, among other great perks.

Click Here To Upgrade

This full story is for paid subscribers.

Sign up for a paid subscription to The Lever now to gain access to this story and more.

Select Your Paid Subscription

Already have an account? Sign in

item.title: The Compliance Officer Who Flagged Epstein — And Lost Her Job
item.description: An FBI interview reveals Deutsche Bank’s internal warnings about Jeffrey Epstein and Jared Kushner — and alleged retaliation against the whistleblower.
item.dc:creator: Veronica Riccobene
item.content:encoded:
A former compliance officer for the international financial powerhouse Deutsche Bank told the Federal Bureau of Investigation she was fired in 2018 after raising concerns about suspicious banking activity from accounts owned by financier and sex predator Jeffrey Epstein, as well as accounts linked to Jared Kushner, President Donald Trump’s son-in-law, adviser, and business partner. The allegations come from an FBI interview report included in the Justice Department’s latest release of Epstein-related documents.

The former anti-money laundering official detailed “mind boggling” cryptocurrency transactions between Kushner’s company and a Russian individual. She also claimed that she was punished after raising concerns about more than 100 politically-connected individuals who had been shielded from typical anti-money laundering reviews.
